Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after work or on weekends, but it rarely happened the way I planned. Most of my 28 hours were scattered throughout the week, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because my anxiety was keeping me up. I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, but I wouldn't call it organized at all.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly panic and try to cram when I remembered something was due. Deadlines didn't even really motivate me that much because by the time I noticed them, it often felt too late to bother. My extracurricular commitments ate into whatever schedule I tried to set.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or a sudden wave of anxiety that made me log in. Sometimes a peer from my support group would mention something about the course, and I'd think 'oh right, that exists.' I rarely logged in with any real purpose—it was more like checking to see how far behind I'd fallen, which usually made me feel worse.
Q4: Not really, no. There was maybe a week early on where I tried to make it a habit, logging in every morning with coffee. But that fell apart quickly. The LMS never became routine for me—it always felt like this thing I was supposed to do but kept avoiding. Every time I opened it, I just saw all the things I hadn't done.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful because I learn better by listening. I could put them on while commuting or lying in bed at night. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in simpler terms than the professor. But honestly, I didn't use most of the resources enough to really judge what was useful.
Q6: I ignored almost all the readings and the practice quizzes. The readings felt overwhelming—walls of text that I couldn't focus on with my anxiety. The quizzes felt pointless because I knew I hadn't learned the material well enough to do them anyway. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it was short, clear, and I could see immediate relevance. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. But most things in the course felt like they required more mental energy than I had available. When you're already feeling sad and anxious, everything feels like too much effort for too little payoff.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have been invisible to me. If something wasn't required, my brain immediately categorized it as 'skip.' Even required things I often skipped, so optional stuff had zero chance. There's this weird thing where having the choice made it easier to choose not to do it.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a bunch of lecture audio files early in the semester and listened to them on my phone. I also had a friend who shared their notes with me, which I'd read through sometimes. A lot of my studying—what little there was—happened away from my computer entirely because sitting at the computer made my anxiety spike.
Q10: There were nights I'd spend hours going over my friend's handwritten notes or rewinding the same audio lecture segment over and over on my phone. None of that showed up anywhere. I also had some long conversations with peers from my support group about course concepts, which helped me understand things better than the LMS ever did.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download the audio lectures and then never go back to the LMS page for that content. I'd listen to the same recording multiple times over several days, but the system would only show that one download. It looked like I barely engaged when actually I was replaying things constantly, just offline.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart. My anxiety got really bad, and I started avoiding anything related to the course because it triggered this spiral of negative thoughts. I was already behind, and looking at how much I'd missed made me feel hopeless. The sadness just kind of took over and made everything feel pointless.
Q13: Fatigue was huge. I was exhausted all the time, partly from the anxiety keeping me up at night. When I was tired, logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and just life stuff meant the course kept getting pushed down the priority list until it basically disappeared.
Q14: All the time. I'd open a video, let it play, and realize twenty minutes later that I'd been staring at the wall thinking about something else entirely. My mind would wander to all the things stressing me out. I'd technically be 'watching' the content but absorbing absolutely nothing. It was like being physically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed everything important. The assignments especially—I knew they mattered, but starting them felt impossible. There was this paralysis where the anxiety about doing it badly was worse than the anxiety about not doing it at all. So I'd avoid it, feel guilty, get more anxious, and avoid it more. A vicious cycle.
Q16: Deadlines should have motivated me, but they mostly just increased my anxiety without actually getting me to act. I'd watch deadlines approach with this sense of dread, then watch them pass with this weird mix of relief and shame. Sometimes I'd log in right before a deadline, see it was too late, and log out feeling worse than before.
Q17: I barely got any feedback because I barely submitted anything. The little feedback I did receive came too late to feel useful—I'd already mentally moved on or given up on that topic. When peers in my support group gave me informal feedback on my understanding, that actually helped more than official course feedback ever did.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid and unforgiving. If you fell behind, there was no easy way to catch up. Everything built on previous material, so missing early stuff meant later stuff made no sense. The structure assumed consistent engagement, which just wasn't possible for me. It felt designed for someone with their life together.
Q19: The lack of flexibility made engagement harder. No extensions, no alternative formats, no acknowledgment that people learn differently or have different life circumstances. If there had been more audio options or shorter modules, maybe I could have kept up. The long reading-heavy assignments were basically impossible for me to complete.
Q20: I think that happens when people are trying but struggling. They're logging in, watching videos, maybe even taking notes—but when it comes to actually producing work, something blocks them. Could be anxiety, could be confusion, could be perfectionism. The activity shows effort, but the submissions show where that effort hits a wall.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Some people are efficient—they know exactly what they need, they get it, they work offline. The LMS doesn't capture studying from textbooks, group study sessions, or just thinking through problems on a walk. Low activity doesn't mean low learning; it might mean different learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked things and for how long. That's about it. They miss whether you understood anything, whether you were actually paying attention, whether you were learning in other ways. They definitely miss the emotional experience—the anxiety, the avoidance, the sadness. Numbers don't capture why someone disengaged.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling capable of learning it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. It's being mentally present, curious, and feeling like your effort matters. For me, engagement was rare this semester—I wanted to care, but something inside me just couldn't connect.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually wanted to succeed but couldn't make myself act on it. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that were my lifeline, or the hours spent with downloaded audio, or the anxiety attacks that kept me from logging in. It wouldn't show that I'm not lazy—I'm struggling. There's a difference.
